t: — neuroleptic-induced catatonia and within the spectrum of NMS; antipsychotic discontinuation cholinergic and adrenergic rebound.
— Autoimmune anti-NMDA-R encephalitis presents a classic catatonic presentation.
DSM-5-TR criteria — at least 3 of 12 symptoms:
— psychomotor activity absence, unresponsiveness to surroundings.
– posture induced by examiner is maintained like "waxy flexibility".
— absence of speech or significant reduction.
— opposition to instructions or external stimuli.
— Motor reduction, mutism dominant; majority of cases.
– hyperthermia with excited form, autonomic instability, high CPK, mortality risk – coincides with NMS; emergency medical condition.
DSM-5-TR — ≥3 of 12 features; ICD-11 — same clinical features across different categories.
1–2 mg IV/IM lorazepam → significant reduction of catatonic signs within 10–30 minutes confirms the diagnosis (Bush G. et al. Acta Psychiatr Scand 1996).
23-item standardized scale; first 14 items screening (≥ 2 signs — positive).
Complete blood count, liver/renal, electrolytes (Na, K, Ca, Mg, P), glucose, ammonia, CPK, troponin, thyroid, B12.
EEG — rule out non-convulsive status epilepticus.
Lumbar puncture — suspected encephalitis / autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDA-R, anti-LGI1 antibodies).
History of antipsychotic use; hyperthermia, rigidity, very high CPK; clinical overlap — some consider the same spectrum.
Brain MRI — frontal or bilateral thalamic pathology.
Anti-NMDA-R, anti-LGI1; CSF findings, MRI, EEG atypical.
The above laboratory and instrumental examinations — an etiological search.
Autonomic function (blood pressure, pulse, temperature, urine output) — especially in malignant form.
Comorbid mood disorder and psychotic symptom assessment (later, when patient can speak).
— particularly typical and high D2 antagonists (haloperidol, risperidone high dose) — can enhance catatonia, transform into malignant form.
1–2 mg IM/IV, titration based on initial response; typical effective dose 6–24 mg/day; some patients require 30+ mg. ~70–80% patients respond (Sienaert P. et al. Front Psychiatry 2014 review).
— refractory cases (lorazepam non-response within 48–72 hours), malignant catatonia — first-line, life-saving measure. NICE TA59 — strong recommendation in catatonia.
— parallel: if against a background of affective disorder, mood stabilizer (lithium); in schizophrenia context — atypical antipsychotic (clozapine or quetiapine less risky) cautiously with lorazepam.
— Immunotherapy (IV steroid, IVIG, plasmapheresis, second-line rituximab).
— Hydration, nutrition (NG tube necessary), DVT prophylaxis, skin care, aspiration pneumonia prophylaxis.
33. — ICU; aggressive hydration, dantrolene, bromocriptine, ECT.
BAP 2023 (British Association for Psychopharmacology) Catatonia Consensus Guidelines
lorazepam is first-line; ECT is for refractory or malignant cases; avoid or use antipsychotics with extreme caution.
Fink M., Taylor M.A. Catatonia: A Clinician's Guide (2003)
— classic protocol; lorazepam test and high-dose lorazepam therapy.
23-item scale; first 14 items screening (≥ 2 positive); 23 items severity. Bush G. et al. Acta Psychiatr Scand 1996.
Reduction of catatonic signs within 10–30 min after 1–2 mg IM/IV lorazepam administration confirms diagnosis and guides therapy plan. Bush G. et al. 1996.
Gold standard in catatonia; UK ECT Review Group Lancet 2003 evidence base; NICE TA59. Typically 6–12 sessions, bilateral target.
38. — IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day × 5 days → IVIG and/or plasmapheresis; in refractory cases rituximab, cyclophosphamide. Graus F. et al. Lancet Neurol 2016 autoimmune encephalitis protocol.
Lorazepam and/or ECT response — most patients achieve full recovery within days to weeks.
High relapse if etiological disorder is untreated.
Complications — aspiration pneumonia from prolonged stupor, DVT, dehydration, skin ulcers.
Monitoring — in etiological disorder context; recognition of relapse markers.
Myth 1: "Catatonia is a subtype of schizophrenia"
DSM-5 (2013) and ICD-11 (2019) — catatonia as a transnosological syndrome; schizophrenia subtype status abolished. More frequent in the context of affective disorders (Fink & Taylor 2003).
Myth 2: "Catatonic patient should be treated with antipsychotics"
Antipsychotic may exacerbate catatonia and lead to malignant form.
. First-line — lorazepam, then ECT. Antipsychotic if needed for the etiological disorder, cautiously with lorazepam; atypical preferred (clozapine or quetiapine).
Myth 3: "A catatonic patient has lost consciousness or is in a coma"

Most catatonic patients are fully aware of surroundings — mutism and psychomotor impairment are NOT loss of consciousness. Patient remembers everything after episode. Staff conversations near patient should be cautious.
Myth 4: "Catatonia is a rare or historical condition, absent in modern psychiatry"
7–15% of patients admitted to psychiatric hospitals have catatonic symptoms (Fink & Taylor 2003); diagnosis is limited — it is an underdiagnosed syndrome.
Myth 5: "Lorazepam only sedates, does not provide 'true' treatment"
Lorazepam targets the core mechanism of catatonic syndrome (GABAergic hypofunction); ~70–80% of patients experience dramatic and rapid clinical recovery.
